answer:
**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)**  
This meta-analysis finds that vocational interests and interest–job fit predict performance, training success, and persistence. It supports taking “interesting work” seriously, but not treating it as the only decision variable.

**“Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?” — Stern (2004)**  
Using data on PhD scientists, the paper finds that researchers accept lower wages for jobs with scientific freedom and the ability to publish. It is direct evidence that interesting, autonomous research work has real monetary value to technical workers, though it describes a tradeoff rather than a universal rule.

**“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
The job characteristics model predicts motivation from skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. A role can be motivating even if the topic is less fascinating when it offers leadership, ownership, and visible impact, but a role low on these dimensions is risky.

**“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000)**  
The paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs for motivation and well-being. This implies you should compare the roles on daily autonomy, mastery, team connection, and growth, not only on topic interestingness or salary.

**“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile (1997)**  
The paper reviews evidence that intrinsic motivation is especially important for creativity, because people explore more deeply when the work itself is engaging. It also distinguishes enabling extrinsic factors from controlling ones, so the lesson is to protect intrinsic motivation rather than ignore pay, resources, or role design.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
This meta-analysis finds that fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. Interesting work is one part of fit, but team quality, manager fit, and organizational environment also matter substantially.

**“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)**  
The meta-analysis finds that controlling tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. It cautions against substituting cash or status for intrinsically motivating work, while not implying that adequate pay or advancement is harmful.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Across studies, pay level is only weakly associated with job satisfaction, while satisfaction with pay has a stronger but still bounded relationship. It suggests a somewhat higher salary should not dominate if both roles already meet your financial and fairness needs.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
This paper reconciles prior conflicting findings by showing that income is associated with higher experienced well-being for most people, but with a plateau among the least happy subgroup. It means money is not irrelevant, but its marginal value should be weighed against daily work quality, autonomy, and meaning.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
The paper finds that people who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher job and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. It supports the value of meaningful work, but meaning can come from mission, craft, leadership, or impact—not only from intellectual interestingness.

**“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003)**  
The paper distinguishes harmonious passion, which integrates well with life and predicts well-being, from obsessive passion, which creates conflict and strain. It argues against focusing on interesting work “almost exclusively” if that crowds out balance, relationships, or broader goals.

**“Understanding Contemporary Forms of Exploitation: Attributions of Passion Serve to Legitimize the Poor Treatment of Workers” — Kim, Campbell, Shepherd & Kay (2020)**  
The studies show that people view poor treatment of passionate workers as more legitimate, including extra work and lower compensation. It is a warning that loving the work can be exploited, so interestingness should not excuse bad role design, unfair pay, or weak career leverage.

**“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue (2019)**  
The study finds that organizations often promote strong individual contributors even when the traits that made them strong contributors do not predict managerial performance. It suggests that leading a team should be evaluated as a distinct job with distinct skills, not automatically as an upgrade from doing interesting research.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.**  
  Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback tend to produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports heavily weighting “interesting work,” especially if interesting means challenging, autonomous, and meaningful.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci, 2005.**  
  Work motivation and well-being are strongest when jobs satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs. The implication is not “interestingness only,” but rather choosing the role that better supports deep ownership, mastery, and human connection.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.**  
  Intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially for complex, creative, and quality-focused work. Extrinsic incentives still matter, so the evidence favors prioritizing interesting work but not ignoring pay and role structure.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile, 1997.**  
  Creativity is strongly linked to intrinsic motivation, especially when people find the work itself engaging and meaningful. For research roles, this is one of the stronger arguments that “less interesting” work can reduce your best output.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Controlling extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, particularly for tasks that are already interesting. Higher salary is not inherently bad, but if the new role feels more externally driven and less autonomous, it may reduce motivation.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.**  
  Actual pay has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, while satisfaction with pay matters more than pay level itself. This suggests a somewhat higher salary may not compensate much for a large loss in work interest.

- **“The Importance of Pay in Employee Motivation: Discrepancies Between What People Say and What They Do” — Rynes, Gerhart & Minette, 2004.**  
  People often understate how much pay affects their choices and motivation. This is a direct caution against focusing almost exclusively on interesting work.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Fit between a person and their job, organization, team, and manager predicts satisfaction, commitment, and performance. The best choice is likely the one with stronger overall fit, not simply the one with the highest novelty or salary.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019.**  
  Meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and performance. This supports prioritizing work that feels important and personally significant, though it does not imply meaning should dominate all other factors.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. This supports choosing work you find deeply engaging, but it also shows that orientation toward the same role can differ by person.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009.**  
  Deeply meaningful work can lead people to accept worse pay, worse conditions, and self-exploitation. This is a warning that “interesting and meaningful” work is valuable, but not a reason to neglect compensation, boundaries, or career leverage.

- **“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages” — Mas & Pallais, 2017.**  
  Workers place substantial monetary value on non-pay job attributes such as flexibility, schedule control, and working conditions. This supports evaluating the full job bundle rather than salary alone.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**  
  Good bosses substantially improve worker productivity, and supervisor quality has large organizational value. If leading a team is a genuine long-term goal, the management opportunity itself may be an important source of growth and impact.

- **“Flow at Work: Evidence for an Upward Spiral of Personal and Organizational Resources” — Salanova, Bakker & Llorens, 2006.**  
  Flow at work—absorption, enjoyment, and intrinsic motivation—is linked to personal resources and positive work outcomes. Highly interesting technical work may be especially valuable if it reliably creates flow.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023.**  
  Emotional well-being generally rises with income, though the pattern differs across people and may flatten for the least happy group. This argues against dismissing salary entirely, while also implying that extra income may not offset a major loss in day-to-day work quality.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce experienced meaningfulness and responsibility, which predict motivation and satisfaction. It supports treating interesting/autonomous work as central, but as part of overall job design rather than the only variable.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000).** Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are key conditions for intrinsic motivation and well-being. A lead role could improve or worsen things depending on whether it increases those needs or mainly reduces the work you find intellectually engaging.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).** Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, while informational feedback can enhance it. This supports the idea that salary/status can be overweighted, though it does not imply compensation is irrelevant.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).** Fit predicts job satisfaction, organizational commitment, turnover intentions, and performance. “Interesting work” is one form of person–job fit, but team, manager, and organizational fit also matter.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).** Pay level is positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. This supports discounting a small salary increase relative to daily work quality, especially when baseline pay is already high.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006).** People tend to overestimate income’s effect on happiness because thinking about income makes it especially salient. The paper supports the claim that day-to-day work experience may be underweighted relative to money.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).** Experienced well-being generally rises with income, but the pattern varies across people and is not a simple universal plateau. This argues against focusing on interestingness almost exclusively, because money can still matter, just not always dominantly.

- **“A Survey Method for Characterizing Daily Life Experience: The Day Reconstruction Method” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2004).** Moment-to-moment well-being depends strongly on actual activities and social context. For job choice, this suggests comparing the lived week-to-week experience of each role rather than abstract prestige, salary, or title.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Examination of the Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being” — Bowling, Eschleman & Wang (2010).** Job satisfaction is strongly related to broader subjective well-being. Because work occupies so much waking time, chronically less interesting work can spill over into life satisfaction.

- **“Happiness and Productivity” — Oswald, Proto & Sgroi (2015).** Experimentally induced happiness increased productivity on paid tasks. Enjoyable work can therefore be instrumentally valuable for output, not merely personally pleasant.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997).** People who experience their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction. The same occupation can be experienced differently by different people, implying that meaning depends partly on framing and role construction.

- **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001).** Employees can reshape tasks, relationships, and interpretations to make work more meaningful. A nominally less interesting leadership role could become more interesting if it offers enough autonomy to craft technical direction and team culture.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue (2019).** Firms often promote strong individual contributors into management even when individual performance poorly predicts managerial performance. This cautions that leading a team is a different job, so wanting leadership should be evaluated against the actual managerial work involved.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015).** Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity through teaching, motivation, and task allocation. Leading a small team can be high-impact and satisfying, but its rewards may come from enabling others rather than doing the most interesting individual technical work.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018).** People with fixed theories of interest are less open to new domains and more likely to disengage when an interest becomes difficult. This cautions against treating current interestingness as a fixed signal that should dominate almost exclusively.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)  
Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback produce higher intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing interesting, self-directed work, but it defines “interesting” broadly rather than as technical novelty alone.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci (2000)  
Well-being and sustained motivation depend strongly on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting work matters, but leading a team could also satisfy competence and relatedness if the environment is good.

**Meaningful Work: A Review and Meta-Analysis** — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and life satisfaction. The evidence favors meaningful/engaging work, but not “interestingness” as the only variable.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. This argues against focusing almost exclusively on task interestingness, because team/org/manager fit also matter a lot.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012)  
People perform better and persist more when their work matches their interests. The effect is real, but moderate, so interest is important rather than overwhelmingly decisive.

**Interest Congruence and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis** — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2017)  
Interest–job fit is positively related to job satisfaction. The paper supports taking “will I find this work interesting?” seriously, but it does not imply ignoring pay, growth, autonomy, or colleagues.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)  
Controlling external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. Higher pay is not automatically bad, but if the new role feels less autonomous or more status-driven, motivation may suffer.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)  
Pay level has only a weak relationship with job satisfaction. This supports not over-weighting a modest salary increase when the work itself and environment differ substantially.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** — Kahneman & Deaton (2010)  
Higher income improves people’s overall life evaluation, while day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in their data. This suggests salary matters, but daily work experience can matter more for lived happiness.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises With Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** — Killingsworth (2021)  
Experienced well-being continues to rise with income, with no sharp plateau in this study. This complicates the “ignore salary” view, though the income effect is gradual and does not dominate job quality.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, & Stone (2006)  
People tend to overestimate how much income and major life circumstances affect happiness because they focus on them when deciding. This supports giving heavy weight to everyday work experience, including whether the work is engaging.

**Psychological Conditions of Personal Engagement and Disengagement at Work** — Kahn (1990)  
Engagement depends on psychological meaningfulness, safety, and availability. Interesting work helps with meaningfulness, but leadership quality, team trust, and workload determine whether people actually engage.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art** — Bakker & Demerouti (2007)  
Burnout and engagement depend on the balance between job demands and resources such as autonomy, feedback, support, and growth. A fascinating role can still be bad if demands are draining, while a less technically exciting role can be good if it provides resources and impact.

**The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions** — Grant (2008)  
Seeing how one’s work benefits others can increase motivation and performance. Leading a small team may be motivating if it creates visible impact, even if the direct technical work is less interesting.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** — O’Keefe, Dweck, & Walton (2018)  
Believing interests are fixed can reduce openness and persistence when new areas become difficult. This argues against assuming the OpenAI role will remain “less interesting” forever, but also against abandoning a role that already fits your interests well.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham (1976).**  
Shows that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. This supports weighting interesting work heavily, but treats “interesting” as one cluster of job-design factors rather than the only factor.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).**  
Finds strong links between motivating work design—autonomy, complexity, significance, feedback—and satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It also shows social support, interdependence, and work context matter, so the evidence does not support optimizing exclusively for intellectual interest.

**Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay (2019).**  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with engagement, job satisfaction, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. This supports taking meaningful or interesting work very seriously, especially once financial needs are met.

**Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020).**  
Vocational interest fit reliably predicts job satisfaction and related career outcomes. This supports your friends’ point that interest fit is important, but fit can include leadership, values, people, and environment—not only technical topic interest.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).**  
People perform better and persist more when their work aligns with their interests. The effect is meaningful but not overwhelming, suggesting interest is a major input rather than an exclusive decision rule.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).**  
Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and complex work. This directly argues against focusing “almost exclusively” on either interestingness or money.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).**  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This suggests salary/status should not dominate the decision, but it does not imply compensation is irrelevant.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).**  
Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, while satisfaction with pay matters somewhat more. For already well-paid professionals, a small salary increase is unlikely to outweigh large differences in daily work quality.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).**  
Experienced well-being generally rises with income, roughly logarithmically, though with variation across people. This argues against ignoring salary entirely, but implies that marginal pay increases may be relatively small compared with role content and fit.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).**  
Person–job, person–organization, person–group, and person–supervisor fit all predict satisfaction, commitment, and turnover. This suggests the right question is broader than “interesting vs. paid more”: it is whether the whole role fits you.

**The “What” and “Why” of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior — Deci & Ryan (2000).**  
Well-being and motivation are higher when work supports autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research may support competence and autonomy, while leading a team may support competence and relatedness if it is genuinely self-chosen.

**Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model — Sheldon & Elliot (1999).**  
People gain more lasting well-being from pursuing goals aligned with their own values and interests than from externally pressured goals. If leading a small team is a deep personal goal, it may be intrinsically valuable even if the technical work is less interesting.

**Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead — Chan & Drasgow (2001).**  
Motivation to lead is a distinct trait-like factor that predicts leadership participation and development. This makes the team-lead opportunity relevant as an intrinsic career interest, not merely as title inflation.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art — Bakker & Demerouti (2007).**  
Job resources such as autonomy, support, and growth opportunities promote engagement, while excessive demands promote burnout. A leadership role could be energizing or draining depending on resources, workload, and control.

**The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work — Bunderson & Thompson (2009).**  
Deeply meaningful work can produce fulfillment, identity, and commitment, but also self-sacrifice and tolerance of worse conditions. This is a caution against treating “interesting work” as an unquestioned trump card.

**The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect — Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005).**  
Job satisfaction often rises after a voluntary move and then partially declines as novelty fades. This cautions against overweighting the immediate appeal of a new company, salary bump, or first leadership title.

I think I need to include citations or at least reference specific papers, like "The job satisfaction–job performance relationship." The question mentions that research shows doing interesting things is underrated, and I'd like to focus on that idea almost exclusively. So, I need to select relevant papers that can support this point effectively. It's important to provide solid backing for my response, so I'll make sure I gather appropriate sources.
**Evaluating career choices**

I think it’s important to choose a role based on what I genuinely want, whether that’s research depth or leadership. It shouldn’t just be about pursuing something that looks prestigious or comes with a high salary. The work by Vallerand et al. in 2003 discusses the balance between obsessive and harmonious passion, which adds context to understanding my motivations. I find that fitting my career to my true passions leads to greater satisfaction!
**Exploring judgment and satisfaction**

I'm thinking about the focusing illusion in judgments of life satisfaction, particularly how it relates to salary and prestige, though not in a direct way. I should include a brief summary of affective forecasting too. For my final answer, I want to include around 15 relevant papers, but I need to ensure it's balanced and doesn’t lean too heavily on just one aspect. Perhaps including around 12 relevant sources would provide a good range.
**Evaluating creativity in environments**

For research-like roles, I think that losing intrinsic curiosity and challenge could reduce both creative output and satisfaction. There’s this study by Amabile et al. that shows creative work environments need challenge, freedom, resources, encouragement, and support to thrive. But if there are organizational pressures, creativity can suffer. It’s really about finding environments that allow for autonomy and creative freedom. Additionally, Sheldon & Elliot highlight that aligning goals with interests can enhance persistence and well-being.
**Analyzing well-being studies**

I'm exploring a potential problem with the Killingsworth 2023 study, which seems to resolve between Kahneman/Deaton and Killingsworth. It states that well-being generally rises with income for most people, but the effect might be small. I guess it’s worth noting it doesn’t support letting salary dominate job satisfaction. The Judge pay satisfaction meta-analysis shows overall job satisfaction correlations with pay at only .15, which could be seen as modest. I'm also considering how work with skill variety contributes to meaningfulness and responsibility, supported by the Hackman/Oldham test.
answer:
- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation robustly predicts performance, especially quality and complex work. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence supports prioritizing interesting work heavily but not “almost exclusively.”

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).** Job features such as autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, social support, and interdependence are strongly associated with satisfaction and motivation. “Interestingness” matters most as part of a broader bundle of job-design features, not as a standalone criterion.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Work that offers skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback creates experienced meaningfulness and responsibility. This supports choosing work that is inherently engaging and autonomous, especially if the alternative reduces those core features.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).** Person–job and person–organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. The implication is to choose the role whose day-to-day tasks, values, team, and manager fit you, rather than optimizing only for interest, title, or pay.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).** People tend to perform better when their vocational interests align with their work. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously because interest fit can compound into performance and career success over time.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).** Pay level is positively related to job and pay satisfaction, but the association with overall job satisfaction is modest. This suggests a somewhat higher salary should matter, but usually less than sustained differences in work content and fit once pay is already high.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).** Emotional well-being generally continues to rise with income for most people, though the effect is relatively small and varies across people. This argues against dismissing salary, but it does not support letting salary dominate over intrinsic fit.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).** Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, while informational or competence-affirming rewards can help. For creative technical work, this warns that a pay/title upgrade is not automatically beneficial if it shifts attention away from curiosity and autonomy.

- **“Motivation and Creativity: Effects of Motivational Orientation on Creative Writers” — Amabile (1985).** Experimental manipulations emphasizing intrinsic motivation produced more creative work than those emphasizing extrinsic reasons. For research-like roles, losing intrinsic curiosity and challenge can plausibly reduce creative output and satisfaction.

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby & Herron (1996).** Creative work environments are characterized by challenge, freedom, resources, encouragement, and supportive teams, while organizational impediments and excessive pressure undermine creativity. This makes the relevant comparison not simply “interesting versus less interesting,” but which environment gives you more autonomy, support, challenge, and creative latitude.

- **“Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model” — Sheldon & Elliot (1999).** People are more likely to persist, attain goals, and gain well-being when their goals are aligned with their interests and values. This supports choosing the role that matches your deeper motives, including whether leading a small team is a genuine self-concordant goal.

- **“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003).** Harmonious passion is associated with flow, positive affect, and adaptive outcomes, whereas obsessive passion is associated with conflict and costs. This cautions against treating “interesting work” as the only criterion if it crowds out leadership aspirations, relationships, health, or broader life goals.

- **“The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review” — Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton (2001).** Job satisfaction and job performance are positively related, contradicting the view that enjoyment is merely a luxury. If one role is much more satisfying day-to-day, that difference may affect both well-being and long-run performance.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015).** Bosses have substantial effects on worker productivity, partly through coaching, task allocation, and motivation. A role involving team leadership may therefore be valuable and intrinsically rewarding if management itself is an interesting skill you want to develop.

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976. Summarize here.
- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007. Summarize here.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976  
Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing interesting/autonomous work, but it defines “interesting” broadly rather than as topic fascination alone.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007  
A large meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, and social support predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. The evidence supports work design as crucial, but not “interestingness” almost exclusively.

**The Relation Between Interest Congruence and Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis** — Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg, 1993  
Matching one’s interests to one’s work environment is positively related to job satisfaction. The effect is real but modest, so interest matters meaningfully without dominating all other factors.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012  
Interest fit predicts job performance, training performance, and persistence, especially when interests align with core job tasks. This is particularly relevant for research-heavy roles where curiosity and sustained engagement drive excellence.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci, 2000  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs underlying motivation and well-being. Interesting work helps when it supports these needs, but leading a team, culture, and relationships can support them too.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014  
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially for complex and quality-focused work. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the literature does not support ignoring pay or role structure entirely.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010  
Pay level has only a weak relationship with overall job satisfaction. A modest salary increase is unlikely to outweigh large differences in work content, autonomy, or fit for most people.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006  
People tend to overestimate how much income differences affect day-to-day happiness because money is salient when making comparisons. This supports the idea that salary and prestige can be overweighted relative to daily work experience.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023  
Experienced well-being generally rises with income for many people, but effects are smaller and more nuanced than simple “more money equals happier” stories suggest. Salary matters, but at already-high income levels it is unlikely to be the sole decisive variable.

**Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want** — Wilson & Gilbert, 2005  
People often mispredict how future situations will feel, especially by overweighting vivid, salient features and underweighting adaptation. In job choice, this means titles, salary, and prestige may loom larger than the repeated experience of doing the work.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005  
Person–job fit, person–organization fit, team fit, and supervisor fit all predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. Interesting work is one kind of fit, but culture, team, and manager fit are independent predictors.

**Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead** — Chan & Drasgow, 2001  
Motivation to lead is a meaningful individual difference associated with leadership emergence and leadership-related outcomes. If leading a team is a deep, persistent goal, the leadership aspect of a role can be a real source of fit rather than a superficial perk.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout** — Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner & Schaufeli, 2001  
Burnout is predicted by high job demands and insufficient resources such as autonomy, support, and feedback. Even highly interesting work can become costly if demands are excessive, while less interesting work can be more sustainable if resources are strong.

answer:
- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis”**  
  This meta-analysis finds that people whose vocational interests fit their work tend to have higher job satisfaction and better work-related outcomes. It supports prioritizing genuinely interesting work, but not treating it as the only variable.

- **“Employee Fit with the Job, Organization, Vocation, and Supervisor: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Environment Fit”**  
  Person–job and person–organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. “Interesting work” is one kind of fit, but team, manager, company, and role fit also matter.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis”**  
  Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interesting research matters, but leading a team could also increase autonomy, significance, and feedback.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”**  
  Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of intrinsic motivation and well-being. The best role may be the one that best satisfies these needs, not necessarily the one with the most intellectually exciting topic.

- **“Meaningful Work and Well-Being: A Meta-Analysis”**  
  Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. This supports taking intrinsic interest and meaning very seriously, while stopping short of “almost exclusively.”

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research”**  
  Interest fit predicts not just satisfaction but also performance and training success. If Meta’s work is much more aligned with your interests, that may translate into better long-term excellence, not merely more enjoyment.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”**  
  Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. A slightly higher salary may matter less than work content, autonomy, and fit, especially once compensation is already high.

- **“Job Satisfaction and Job Performance: A Meta-Analysis”**  
  Job satisfaction and job performance are positively related. Choosing work you find satisfying can be strategically rational for career growth, not just emotionally appealing.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work”**  
  People who see work as a calling often accept worse pay, longer hours, and more sacrifice because the work feels meaningful. This cautions against over-optimizing for interesting or meaningful work if it leads you to ignore material costs.

- **“Job Burnout”**  
  Burnout is driven by mismatches in workload, control, reward, community, fairness, and values. Interesting work does not fully protect against burnout, and a less interesting role with better control or support can sometimes be healthier.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?”**  
  People who believe interests are fixed are more likely to abandon valuable paths when they become difficult or initially less exciting. A management role that seems less interesting now could become interesting if it develops important new motives and skills.

- **“Focalism: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting”**  
  People overestimate how much one salient difference will affect future happiness because they focus too narrowly on it. You may be over-weighting “interesting research” or “team leadership” while under-weighting daily meetings, manager quality, autonomy, coworkers, and pace.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle”**  
  High performers in individual-contributor roles are often promoted into management, but IC excellence does not reliably predict managerial excellence. Wanting to lead is important evidence, but management fit should be evaluated separately from research ability.

- **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work”**  
  Employees can reshape tasks, relationships, and the meaning of their jobs to increase fit and motivation. Before deciding, consider whether you can craft more leadership at Meta or craft more intellectual interest into the OpenAI role.

  Flow is most likely when challenge and skill are both high, producing deep absorption and intrinsic reward. This supports valuing difficult, interesting research work if it reliably creates that state.

- **“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant, 2008.**  
  Seeing how one’s work benefits others can increase persistence and performance. This suggests a less technically interesting leadership role could still be motivating if it creates visible impact through people, product, or mission.

- **“Work Engagement: A Quantitative Review and Test of Its Relations with Task and Contextual Performance” — Christian, Garza, & Slaughter, 2011.**

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** This paper shows that meaningfulness, autonomy, feedback, skill variety, and task significance strongly shape motivation and satisfaction. It supports taking “interesting work” seriously, but it treats interest as one part of a broader job-design bundle.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).** This meta-analysis finds that motivating work characteristics—especially autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback—predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It does not support focusing almost exclusively on interestingness, because social context, working conditions, and role design also matter.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000).** The paper argues that well-being and high-quality motivation come from autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research may satisfy these needs, but leading a small team could also satisfy them if the role is well designed.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation is especially important for complex, creative, and high-quality work. However, extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence favors balancing intrinsic interest with compensation and career structure.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).** Tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation when they feel controlling, especially for already-interesting tasks. This supports caution about choosing a role mainly for pay or title if it makes the work feel less self-directed.

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).** Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. A somewhat higher salary may matter, but it is unlikely to compensate fully for a large loss in work interest or fit.

- **“The Importance of Pay in Employee Motivation: Discrepancies between What People Say and What They Do” — Rynes, Gerhart & Minette (2004).** People often understate how much pay affects their choices, while behavior shows pay can strongly influence attraction and retention. This cuts against the claim that one should ignore compensation almost entirely.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).** Fit with the job and organization strongly predicts satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. The relevant question is not only “which work is more interesting,” but which role better fits your values, skills, desired identity, and environment.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997).** People who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher job and life satisfaction. This supports the importance of meaningful, intrinsically engaging work, but also shows that people differ in whether they primarily seek a job, career, or calling.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009).** Deeply meaningful work can produce identity, commitment, and satisfaction. It can also make people accept sacrifices, lower rewards, or exploitation, so passion is valuable but not safely maximized in isolation.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).** People perform better and persist more when their interests match their work. This supports prioritizing intellectually interesting work, especially in research-heavy roles.

- **“Meta-Analysis of the Relationship between Congruence and Well-Being Measures” — Assouline & Meir (1987).** Interest–environment congruence is positively related to satisfaction and well-being, though the effect is not overwhelming. This suggests that interestingness and fit matter, but they are not the only determinants of career welfare.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth (2021).** Higher income is associated with higher experienced well-being even at relatively high income levels. The effect is typically logarithmic, so a small raise at already-high compensation may be less important than a large change in work quality.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).** This paper finds that income keeps mattering for many people, but the relationship varies and may plateau for the least happy individuals. It argues against dismissing salary, while also implying that income will not fix a fundamentally poor daily work experience.

- **“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want” — Wilson & Gilbert (2005).** People often mispredict how future situations will make them feel because they overfocus on salient features and underweight adaptation and context. In your case, both “more interesting research” and “higher salary plus leadership” could be over- or underweighted.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006).** People often overestimate the happiness impact of income when income is made salient. This is relevant to a salary bump, but the same focusing-illusion logic can also apply to prestige, title, or project interestingness.

**Identifying relevant papers**

Additionally, Hackman & Oldham's job characteristics theory and the Work Design Questionnaire by Morgeson & Humphrey are crucial. **Listing additional research papers**

I need to gather more research papers relevant to work preferences and career success. I should include the Work Preference Inventory, Grant's study on task significance from 2008, and Wrzesniewski's work on jobs, careers, and callings from 1997. answer:
**“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976**  
Shows that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation, satisfaction, and performance. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously, especially when it includes autonomy and meaningful technical challenge.

**“Work Design and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analytic Review” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**  
Finds that motivational job characteristics are strongly related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions. The evidence suggests work content often matters more than people expect, but not to the exclusion of social, organizational, and reward factors.

**“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**  
Finds only a modest association between pay level and job satisfaction. This implies that once compensation is already high, a somewhat higher salary may have limited impact compared with job content, fit, and growth.

**“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**  
Shows that income is more strongly related to life evaluation than to day-to-day emotional well-being, with diminishing returns for emotional well-being at higher incomes. For someone already highly paid, this weakens the case for prioritizing salary increases alone.

**“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021**  
Finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income on a log scale, challenging a simple plateau story. The effect is real but still leaves open whether a marginal salary increase beats changes in work interest, autonomy, or role identity.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023**  
Reconciles earlier findings by showing that income keeps helping many people, while unhappy people may experience a plateau. This suggests money matters, but its marginal value depends on the person’s baseline well-being and circumstances.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**  
Finds that person–job fit and person–organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover. This is directly relevant because “interesting research” and “leading a team” are both forms of fit, not merely perks.

**“Subjective Person–Organization Fit: Bridging the Gap between Conceptualization and Measurement” — Cable & DeRue, 2002**  
Distinguishes needs–supplies fit, demands–abilities fit, and values fit as separate predictors of work attitudes. The paper suggests you should compare whether each role satisfies your needs, uses your strengths, and matches your values rather than optimizing one dimension.

**“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci, 2000**  
Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core drivers of intrinsic motivation and well-being. Interesting research may satisfy competence and autonomy, while leading a team may satisfy competence and relatedness if the environment supports it.

**“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999**  
Finds that some contingent external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. The takeaway is not “ignore money,” but that extrinsic incentives are a poor substitute for genuinely engaging work.

**“The Power of Small Wins” — Amabile & Kramer, 2011**  
Shows that perceived progress in meaningful work is a major driver of positive inner work life and motivation. A role that is less interesting but offers leadership could still be motivating if it creates frequent meaningful progress and ownership.

**“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant, 2008**  
Finds that seeing the impact of one’s work on others can increase motivation and performance. This suggests “interesting” is not only technical novelty; perceived impact and usefulness can also make work motivating.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997**  
Shows that people differ in whether they experience work mainly as a job, career, or calling. If you experience research as a calling, moving to less interesting work may carry a larger psychological cost than salary-based models predict.

**“Wanting, Liking, and Satisfaction” — Hsee & Hastie, 2006**  
Reviews evidence that people often choose based on what they think they will want, not what they will actually like. This is relevant because salary and title are easy to evaluate before joining, while day-to-day interest is easier to underestimate.

**“Affective Forecasting” — Wilson & Gilbert, 2003**  
Shows that people are often inaccurate at predicting future emotional reactions to life changes. This cautions against assuming that either “more interesting research” or “team leadership plus higher pay” will feel exactly as expected.

**“Career Success in a Boundaryless Career World” — Arthur, Khapova & Wilderom, 2005**  
Distinguishes objective career success, such as salary and promotions, from subjective career success, such as meaning, identity, and satisfaction. The paper implies that the better choice depends on which form of success you are optimizing for, not on a universal rule.

answer:
- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation”** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999  
  Across experiments, expected tangible rewards tended to reduce free-choice intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks people already found interesting. This supports the idea that replacing intrinsically motivating work with primarily extrinsic incentives can have hidden costs, though rewards that signal competence or autonomy are less harmful.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”** — Ryan & Deci, 2000  
  The paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs behind motivation and well-being. It implies that interesting, self-directed work can matter more than salary alone, but also that team, manager, and autonomy conditions are central.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory”** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976  
  This classic job-design paper links skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback to internal motivation and job satisfaction. “Interesting work” maps strongly onto these dimensions, but the theory is broader than interest alone.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007  
  This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, feedback, task significance, and social support are strongly associated with satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It supports prioritizing day-to-day work quality, while also showing that social context and responsibility matter.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005  
  Person-job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to quit. If “interesting research” reflects unusually high fit, the evidence suggests losing that fit could be costly.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research”** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012  
  This review finds that people perform better and persist more when their work matches their interests. It supports taking intellectual interest seriously as a career input, not merely as a luxury.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work”** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997  
  People who experience their work as a calling report greater satisfaction and meaning than those who view it mainly as a job or career ladder. If current research feels close to a calling, the paper suggests that compensation and title may not substitute fully.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010  
  Pay level is positively related to pay satisfaction, but only weakly related to overall job satisfaction. This weakens the case for choosing a job mainly because of a modestly higher salary, especially once pay is already high.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being”** — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010  
  This paper finds that income is strongly related to life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns at higher incomes in their data. It suggests that salary increases may affect how people judge their life more than how they feel during workdays.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved”** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023  
  This paper reconciles earlier findings by showing that emotional well-being generally continues rising with income, but with different patterns for happier and less happy people. It argues against saying money stops mattering, while still implying that marginal income is only one part of well-being.

- **“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages”** — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger, 2023

- **“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want”** — Wilson & Gilbert, 2005  
  This review shows that people often mispredict how future events will affect their happiness, partly because they overfocus on salient features. It cautions against focusing exclusively on any single attribute, including interestingness, salary, prestige, or managing a team.

I'm looking into research on expected versus experienced utility, particularly focusing on the "focusing illusion" from Kahneman et al., which shows that people often overestimate the happiness boost from income. I'll mention several relevant papers, including Diener and Biswas-Diener's work on time, money, and well-being, and other studies exploring how income relates to life evaluation and emotional well-being. I’ve discovered that for most people, happiness tends to increase linearly with logarithmic income, which is interesting! **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007).**  
A large meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity are strongly related to job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. This supports prioritizing engaging work, but “interesting” is only one part of a broader work-design bundle.

**“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).**  
This foundational paper argues that meaningful, autonomous, feedback-rich work produces higher internal motivation and satisfaction. It supports the idea that the structure and intrinsic content of the job matter a lot, not merely compensation or title.

**“The Differential Effects of Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivation on Employee Attitudes and Behaviors: A Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014).**  
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality and positive work attitudes, while extrinsic incentives are more associated with performance quantity. The implication is that interesting, self-motivating work matters substantially, especially in creative or research-heavy roles.

**“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000).**  
The paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs that drive motivation and well-being. Interesting work helps mainly when it also satisfies these needs, so “interesting” alone is not the whole story.

**“The Relation between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010).**  
This meta-analysis finds that pay level is only modestly related to job satisfaction. It suggests that a somewhat higher salary is unlikely to dominate day-to-day work experience unless the financial difference materially changes your life.

**“The Relation between Interest Congruence and Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis” — Tranberg, Slane, & Ekeberg (1993).**  
This meta-analysis finds that fit between a person’s interests and their work is positively but modestly related to satisfaction. It supports caring about interesting work, but not focusing on it almost exclusively.

**“A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005).**  
Person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. This implies that fit with the role, team, manager, and organization may matter as much as the abstract interestingness of the work.

**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012).**  
Vocational interests predict job and training performance, especially when the work environment matches the person’s interests. The evidence supports choosing work you find intellectually engaging, particularly in high-skill domains.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997).**  
People who experience their work as a calling report greater satisfaction and meaning than those who see it mainly as a job or career. However, the paper distinguishes meaning from advancement and pay, rather than saying meaning should override all other factors.

**“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, & Stone (2006).**  
People tend to overestimate how much income differences affect moment-to-moment happiness because money becomes salient when they think about life satisfaction. This supports being cautious about overweighting salary in a career decision.

**“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth (2021).**  
This paper finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, though roughly logarithmically rather than explosively. It weakens the claim that salary barely matters, but still suggests that non-monetary job quality can easily dominate modest pay differences.

**“The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout” — Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner, & Schaufeli (2001).**  
Burnout is predicted by high demands and insufficient resources, including lack of autonomy, support, and recovery. Interesting work can protect motivation, but a role with poor demands, politics, or stress can still become bad.

**“Differential Challenge Stressor–Hindrance Stressor Relationships with Job Attitudes, Turnover Intentions, Turnover, and Withdrawal Behavior: A Meta-Analysis” — Podsakoff, LePine, & LePine (2007).**  
Challenge stressors can be associated with motivation and achievement, while hindrance stressors are consistently harmful. Leading a small team could be positive if it feels like challenge and growth, but negative if it mainly adds bureaucracy and blockers.

**“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003).**  
The paper distinguishes harmonious passion, which supports well-being, from obsessive passion, which can produce conflict and burnout. It suggests that pursuing interesting work is good when it remains integrated with the rest of life, not when it becomes the only criterion.
